3: Global livestock production is rapidly increasing, with total meat production expected to be 455 million tonnes by 2050, compared to 258 million tonnes in 2005/2007 (Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012). An increase in meat production will lead to a proportional increase in the amount of manure. This can be perceived as both a positive and a negative development. On the positive side, manure contains nutrients that are crucial for crop growth and it can therefore be seen as a valuable fertiliser. Soil quality can be main- tained or improved with the use of manure instead of mineral fertiliser due to the presence of organic matter in manure. On the other hand, natural resources are needed to handle and transport manure, and emissions to the environment occur during manure storage and field application. More than half of the world's pork production originates from intensive systems, which are mainly

located in eastern North America, western Europe, and Southeast and East Asia (Steinfeld et al., 2006). These areas are known for their high livestock density and problems with nutrient losses, especially nitrogen (N), and soil accumulation, especially phos- phorus (P). Manure treatment technologies are continually being developed in order to decrease the overall environmental impacts of manure. However, it is important to analyse whether these im- pacts are being decreased or technologies are just leading to burden shifting and having impacts in other life cycle stages or impact categories instead.

Slurry acidification is a technology that is used to decrease ammonia (NH3) and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from the slurry management chain (Hou et al., 2015). Decreasing the pH of slurry decreases the dissociation of NH4, and therefore decreases NH3 emissions (Fangueiro et al., 2015). The acid-base equilibrium concentration of NH3(aq) decreases from 1.8% at pH 7.5 to 0.02% at pH 5.5, which in turn decreases the potential for NH3 volatilisation due to the NH3(aq) NH3(g) equilibrium. Examples of additives that can be used for effective slurry acidification include H2SO4, HCI and HNO3. Slurry can be acidified during each of the three major

management steps: in the animal house, during storage and during field application. In-house acidification involves pumping acidified slurry into the storage area beneath slatted floors. The additive is applied on a daily or weekly basis. Acidifying the slurry early in the management chain leads to decreases in NH3 emissions from both animal housing and slurry storage and after field application. Ammonia emissions from pig housing are decreased <70% when slurry is acidified in-house, and by an additional 67% during field application by band-spreading (Kai et al., 2008). This implies that a larger share of N ends up in the soil, which in turn results in an increased mineral N fertiliser value compared to untreated slurry (Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). Another effect of early acidification is that microbial activity in the slurry is greatly decreased, leading to lower CH4 and N20 emissions during storage (Berg et al., 2006; Ottosen et al., 2009; Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). Acidification during storage is typically performed shortly before the slurry is removed from the storage tank. This is a cheaper approach than in- house acidification, but there can be problems with foaming during acid addition (Fangueiro et al., 2015). Acidification during field application of slurry is the final option. The acid is applied in-line on the slurry tanker, shortly before the slurry leaves the tanker. Acidification at the end of storage and at field application decreases NH3 emissions in the field <58%, but does not affect emissions from animal housing and slurry storage (Nyord et al., 2013).

Freshwater eutrophication potential is mainly caused by P losses to the aquatic environment. Intensive livestock production has led to P surpluses on a large proportion of farms in Denmark, as cattle and pig manure contain relatively large amounts of In other areas, on many farms, agricultural fields do not receive sufficient P and farmers rely completely on the application of mineral fertilisers to their fields. The over-fertilisation in some areas leads to P leaching and run-off which can pollute surface waterbodies (Christensen et al., 2013). In addition, phosphorus is a non-renewable resource for which there is no substitute (Scherer and Pfister, 2015). Syers et al. (2008) proposed three reasons for increasing efficiency in the use of P fertiliser: phosphate rock is a non-renewable resource with a limited stock, many soils in developing countries need an improvement in status, and the loss of soil P is a major contributor to freshwater eutrophication. The large amounts of manure particularly increase the heavy metal content of Cu and Zn.

In order to enhance nutrient re-distribution, slurry treatment technologies have been developed that focus on the separation of slurry into solid and liquid fractions. The liquid fraction contains most of the easily available N, but less than half of the P, so is mainly valued as an N fertiliser. The solid fraction is easier to transport, due to its relatively low water content. It has a high concentration of slowly available N and P, so it is mainly valuable as a P fertiliser. Many technologies can be applied for separation, e.g. drum filter, screw press, decanter centrifuge, sieving and natural settling (Foged et al., 2011).

The separation of in-house acidified pig slurry has advantages and disadvantages compared with separation alone. On the one hand, the volume of the liquid fraction increases if slurry is acidified before separation, making the solid fraction less voluminous (Cocolo et al., 2013). On the other hand, a larger share of macro-a micro-nutrients ends up in the liquid fraction, resulting in a smaller amount of P to be removed from the farm (Cocolo et al., 2013). Fangueiro et al. (2010) observed a decrease in N20 emissions from acidified liquid and solid fractions that are not observed for unac- idified liquid and solid fractions nor for unseparated acidified slurry. Acidification of the liquid fraction after centrifuge separation has the advantage of reducing NH3 emissions from this fraction, compared to no acidification. Less strong acid is needed than when unseparated slurry is acidified. However, only emissions from the liquid fraction are decreased, not emissions from the solid fraction.

Currently there is no regulation that limits the amount of P that can be applied from animal manure in most European countries (Amery and Schoumans, 2014). In Denmark there are strict regu- lations on maximum N application for crops, but no rules for P. This is likely to change in the near future and may significantly increase the motivation for farmers to apply technologies that ensure more efficient distribution of manure P.

The aim of this study was to investigate the environmental ef- fects of slurry acidification and slurry separation, and whether there were synergetic environmental benefits from combining these technologies. Furthermore, an analysis was performed as to whether the implementation of regulations restricting P applica- tion rates to soils influenced the environmental impacts of the two technologies.

Decreasing the pH of slurry decreases the dissociation of NH4, and therefore decreases NH3 emissions (Fangueiro et al., 2015). The acid-base equilibrium concentration of NH3(aq) decreases from 1.8% at pH 7.5 to 0.02% at pH 5.5, which in turn decreases the potential for NH3 volatilisation due to the NH3(aq) NH3(g) equilibrium. Examples of additives that can be used for effective slurry acidification include H2SO4, HCI and HNO3. Slurry can be acidified during each of the three major

management steps: in the animal house, during storage and during field application. In-house acidification involves pumping acidified slurry into the storage area beneath slatted floors. The additive is applied on a daily or weekly basis. Acidifying the slurry early in the management chain leads to decreases in NH3 emissions from both animal housing and slurry storage and after field application. Ammonia emissions from pig housing are decreased <70% when slurry is acidified in-house, and by an additional 67% during field application by band-spreading (Kai et al., 2008). This implies that a larger share of N ends up in the soil, which in turn results in an increased mineral N fertiliser value compared to untreated slurry (Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). Another effect of early acidification is that microbial activity in the slurry is greatly decreased, leading to lower CH4 and N20 emissions during storage (Berg et al., 2006; Ottosen et al., 2009; Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). Acidification during storage is typically performed shortly before the slurry is removed from the storage tank. This is a cheaper approach than in- house acidification, but there can be problems with foaming during acid addition (Fangueiro et al., 2015). Acidification during field application of slurry is the final option. The acid is applied in-line on the slurry tanker, shortly before the slurry leaves the tanker. Acidification at the end of storage and at field application decreases NH3 emissions in the field <58%, but does not affect emissions from animal housing and slurry storage (Nyord et al., 2013).

Freshwater eutrophication potential is mainly caused by P losses to the aquatic environment. Intensive livestock production has led to P surpluses on a large proportion of farms in Denmark, as cattle and pig manure contain relatively large amounts of In other areas, on many farms, agricultural fields do not receive sufficient P and farmers rely completely on the application of mineral fertilisers to their fields. The over-fertilisation in some areas leads to P leaching and run-off which can pollute surface waterbodies (Christensen et al., 2013). In addition, phosphorus is a non-renewable resource for which there is no substitute (Scherer and Pfister, 2015). Syers et al. (2008) proposed three reasons for increasing efficiency in the use of P fertiliser: phosphate rock is a non-renewable resource with a limited stock, many soils in developing countries need an improvement in status, and the loss of soil P is a major contributor to freshwater eutrophication. The large amounts of manure particularly increase the heavy metal content of Cu and Zn.

In order to enhance nutrient re-distribution, slurry treatment technologies have been developed that focus on the separation of slurry into solid and liquid fractions. The liquid fraction contains most of the easily available N, but less than half of the P, so is mainly valued as an N fertiliser. The solid fraction is easier to transport, due to its relatively low water content. It has a high concentration of slowly available N and P, so it is mainly valuable as a P fertiliser. Many technologies can be applied for separation, e.g. drum filter, screw press, decanter centrifuge, sieving and natural settling (Foged et al., 2011).

The separation of in-house acidified pig slurry has advantages and disadvantages compared with separation alone. On the one hand, the volume of the liquid fraction increases if slurry is acidified before separation, making the solid fraction less voluminous (Cocolo et al., 2013). On the other hand, a larger share of macro-a micro-nutrients ends up in the liquid fraction, resulting in a smaller amount of P to be removed from the farm (Cocolo et al., 2013). Fangueiro et al. (2010) observed a decrease in N20 emissions from acidified liquid and solid fractions that are not observed for unac- idified liquid and solid fractions nor for unseparated acidified slurry. Acidification of the liquid fraction after centrifuge separation has the advantage of reducing NH3 emissions from this fraction, compared to no acidification. Less strong acid is needed than when unseparated slurry is acidified. However, only emissions from the liquid fraction are decreased, not emissions from the solid fraction.

Currently there is no regulation that limits the amount of P that can be applied from animal manure in most European countries (Amery and Schoumans, 2014). In Denmark there are strict regu- lations on maximum N application for crops, but no rules for P. This is likely to change in the near future and may significantly increase the motivation for farmers to apply technologies that ensure more efficient distribution of manure P.

The aim of this study was to investigate the environmental ef- fects of slurry acidification and slurry separation, and whether there were synergetic environmental benefits from combining these technologies. Furthermore, an analysis was performed as to whether the implementation of regulations restricting P applica- tion rates to soils influenced the environmental impacts of the two technologies.

11: A life cycle assessment (LCA) approach was taken in this study to determine the environmental impact potential of combining slurry acidification and separation and to compare them with the envi- ronmental profiles of no treatment, and treatment with acidifica- tion or separation individually, under both an N regulation regime and a P regulation regime.

The LCA framework and methodology used in this study com- plied with ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 standards (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006). Wherever possible, system expansion was used to avoid allocation. The software used for the LCA modelling was EASETECH (Clavreul et al., 2014) and the Ecoinvent database 2010 V2.2 was used for background processes (Althaus et al., 2007; Nemecek and Kagi, 2007). The functional unit that formed the basis for assessment was the management of 1000 kg of slurry excreted by fattening pigs under prevailing Danish conditions. All resources and emissions are expressed per functional unit in order to ensure an equal basis for comparison of the different manure management scenarios.
12: The geographical scope for the slurry management processes (treatment, slurry storage in animal housing, storage in outdoor facilities and field application) was Denmark, and the analysed technologies were contemporary. This means that data on slurry composition, emissions to the environment, slurry treatment con- ditions, machinery, transportation distances, crops, soil profiles, temperatures and regulations are representative for Denmark. Background processes that occur outside Denmark (e.g. mineral fertiliser production) were also included. Emissions and resource consumptions were analysed from the moment slurry was excreted by finishing pigs to 100 years after field application. The effects of emissions that contribute to climate change were considered for a period of 100 years from the moment they reached the atmosphere.
13: The LCA included resource use and environmental impact po- tentials associated with indoor and outdoor storage and field application of slurry and its treatment fractions, field processes, transportation between the different life cycle stages, production and use of electricity, sulphuric acid, and agricultural lime, and the production, transportation and application of mineral fertiliser (Fig. 1). Processes not included were the production of fattening pigs and its related impacts, and the physical buildings and

2.3.2. Scenarios In this study, five treatment scenarios were considered and compared with a reference scenario in which slurry was not acid- ified or separated:

<list>No separation and no acidification scenario (NS NA): conven- tional slurry management 
No separation and field acidification scenario (NS FA): identical to NS NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the slurry to the field (Nyord et al., 2013; VERA, 2012) 
No separation and in-house acidification scenario (NS HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by outdoor storage and land application of the acidified slurry</list>
using a trailing hose system (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2011; Infarm A/S, 2015)

<list>Separation scenario, no acidification (S NA): slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge, with application of the liquid frac- tion on the pig farm, and transportation and application of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~ 100 km away 
Separation combined with field acidification of the liquid frac- tion scenario S FA): identical to S NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the liquid fraction to the field 
In-house acidification and separation scenario (S HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge. Application of the liquid fraction on the pig farm, and transportation and appli- cation of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~100 km away.</list>

Each of these scenarios was modelled under regulations limiting N application and under regulations limiting both N and P appli- cation, resulting in 12 different combinations of scenarios and regulatory regimes. The scenarios are described in further detail,
including assumptions being made, in the Supplementary material.
14: The LCA included resource use and environmental impact po- tentials associated with indoor and outdoor storage and field application of slurry and its treatment fractions, field processes, transportation between the different life cycle stages, production and use of electricity, sulphuric acid, and agricultural lime, and the production, transportation and application of mineral fertiliser (Fig. 1). 2.3.2. Scenarios In this study, five treatment scenarios were considered and compared with a reference scenario in which slurry was not acid- ified or separated:

<list>No separation and no acidification scenario (NS NA): conven- tional slurry management 
No separation and field acidification scenario (NS FA): identical to NS NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the slurry to the field (Nyord et al., 2013; VERA, 2012) 
No separation and in-house acidification scenario (NS HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by outdoor storage and land application of the acidified slurry</list>
using a trailing hose system (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2011; Infarm A/S, 2015)

<list>Separation scenario, no acidification (S NA): slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge, with application of the liquid frac- tion on the pig farm, and transportation and application of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~ 100 km away 
Separation combined with field acidification of the liquid frac- tion scenario S FA): identical to S NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the liquid fraction to the field 
In-house acidification and separation scenario (S HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge. Application of the liquid fraction on the pig farm, and transportation and appli- cation of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~100 km away.</list>

Each of these scenarios was modelled under regulations limiting N application and under regulations limiting both N and P appli- cation, resulting in 12 different combinations of scenarios and regulatory regimes. The scenarios are described in further detail,
including assumptions being made, in the Supplementary material.

equipment used for the treatment, storage and application of slurry fractions to arable land. As slurry is a waste product from pig pro- duction, no foreseeable changes to pig production are expected from the treatment of slurry. Furthermore, pig production was equal in all analysed scenarios.
2.3.2. Scenarios In this study, five treatment scenarios were considered and compared with a reference scenario in which slurry was not acid- ified or separated:

<list>No separation and no acidification scenario (NS NA): conven- tional slurry management 
No separation and field acidification scenario (NS FA): identical to NS NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the slurry to the field (Nyord et al., 2013; VERA, 2012) 
No separation and in-house acidification scenario (NS HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by outdoor storage and land application of the acidified slurry</list>
using a trailing hose system (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2011; Infarm A/S, 2015)

<list>Separation scenario, no acidification (S NA): slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge, with application of the liquid frac- tion on the pig farm, and transportation and application of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~ 100 km away 
Separation combined with field acidification of the liquid frac- tion scenario S FA): identical to S NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the liquid fraction to the field 
In-house acidification and separation scenario (S HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge. Application of the liquid fraction on the pig farm, and transportation and appli- cation of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~100 km away.</list>

Each of these scenarios was modelled under regulations limiting N application and under regulations limiting both N and P appli- cation, resulting in 12 different combinations of scenarios and regulatory regimes. The scenarios are described in further detail,
including assumptions being made, in the Supplementary material.
19: The LCA included resource use and environmental impact po- tentials associated with indoor and outdoor storage and field application of slurry and its treatment fractions, field processes, transportation between the different life cycle stages, production and use of electricity, sulphuric acid, and agricultural lime, and the production, transportation and application of mineral fertiliser (Fig. 1). 2.3.2. Scenarios In this study, five treatment scenarios were considered and compared with a reference scenario in which slurry was not acid- ified or separated:

<list>No separation and no acidification scenario (NS NA): conven- tional slurry management 
No separation and field acidification scenario (NS FA): identical to NS NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the slurry to the field (Nyord et al., 2013; VERA, 2012) 
No separation and in-house acidification scenario (NS HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by outdoor storage and land application of the acidified slurry</list>
using a trailing hose system (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2011; Infarm A/S, 2015)

<list>Separation scenario, no acidification (S NA): slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge, with application of the liquid frac- tion on the pig farm, and transportation and application of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~ 100 km away 
Separation combined with field acidification of the liquid frac- tion scenario S FA): identical to S NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the liquid fraction to the field 
In-house acidification and separation scenario (S HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge. Application of the liquid fraction on the pig farm, and transportation and appli- cation of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~100 km away.</list>

Each of these scenarios was modelled under regulations limiting N application and under regulations limiting both N and P appli- cation, resulting in 12 different combinations of scenarios and regulatory regimes. The scenarios are described in further detail,
including assumptions being made, in the Supplementary material.

Separation combined with field acidification of the liquid frac- tion scenario S FA): identical to S NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the liquid fraction to the field

Application of the liquid fraction on the pig farm, and transportation and appli- cation of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~100 km away.</list>

Each of these scenarios was modelled under regulations limiting N application and under regulations limiting both N and P appli- cation, resulting in 12 different combinations of scenarios and regulatory regimes. The scenarios are described in further detail,
including assumptions being made, in the Supplementary material.

2.3.2. Scenarios In this study, five treatment scenarios were considered and compared with a reference scenario in which slurry was not acid- ified or separated:

<list>No separation and no acidification scenario (NS NA): conven- tional slurry management 
No separation and field acidification scenario (NS FA): identical to NS NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the slurry to the field (Nyord et al., 2013; VERA, 2012) 
No separation and in-house acidification scenario (NS HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by outdoor storage and land application of the acidified slurry</list>
using a trailing hose system (Danish Environmental Protection Agency, 2011; Infarm A/S, 2015)

<list>Separation scenario, no acidification (S NA): slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge, with application of the liquid frac- tion on the pig farm, and transportation and application of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~ 100 km away 
Separation combined with field acidification of the liquid frac- tion scenario S FA): identical to S NA, apart from the addition of sulphuric acid during application of the liquid fraction to the field 
In-house acidification and separation scenario (S HA): daily acidification of slurry during in-house storage, followed by slurry separation with a decanter centrifuge. Application of the liquid fraction on the pig farm, and transportation and appli- cation of the solid fraction to an arable farm ~100 km away.</list>

Each of these scenarios was modelled under regulations limiting N application and under regulations limiting both N and P appli- cation, resulting in 12 different combinations of scenarios and regulatory regimes. The scenarios are described in further detail,
including assumptions being made, in the Supplementary material.
24: The life cycle impact assessment was conducted in accordance with the recommendations of the ILCD guidelines (Hauschild et al., 2013). Five impact categories appeared most affected by changes in slurry management and were therefore analysed. These are described in the results section: climate change potential in CO2- equivalents (kg CO2-eq), terrestrial eutrophication potential in Accumulated Exceedance, marine water eutrophication potential in N-equivalents (kg N-eq), freshwater eutrophication potential in P- equivalents P-eq), and fossil resource depletion potential in MJ. Furthermore, human toxicity potential and ecotoxicity potential, both in comparative toxic units (CTU), were analysed.
25: A sensitivity analysis was performed in order to analyse the effects of certain parameters. First, the lowest and highest possible values for NH3 emission coefficients from housing and field appli- cation of acidified and non-acidified slurry and the separation fractions were estimated based on the literature (Table S7). A sce- nario analysis was performed in which all the low values were used at the same time to evaluate the result of low NH3 emissions, and all the high estimates were used to test the effect of high emissions. Second, P loss rates were halved to 2.5% of the P surplus and doubled to 10% of the surplus. Third, the choice of inclusion of in- direct N20 emissions was analysed. In the main part of this study, indirect N20 emissions were excluded from the analysis. However, their contribution to climate change potential might be significant for the scenarios analysed. Therefore, a sensitivity analysis was performed on the choice of inclusion or exclusion of indirect N20 emissions. The IPCC (2006) recommends the inclusion of indirect N20 emissions resulting from degradation processes of NH3, NO and NO3 emissions to the environment. A default emission factor of 0.01 kg N2O-N (kg NH3-N, kg NOx-N and NO3-N)-¹ as proposed by the IPCC (2006), was applied in the sensitivity analysis.
26: 3.1. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under an N regulatory regime

Pig slurry acidification and separation showed very different environmental impact potentials (Fig. 2). Slurry separation alone showed a climate change potential 50% lower than the scenario without separation and acidification (Fig. 2a). This decrease was mainly due to larger savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser in the case of slurry separation, which outweighed a positive contribution to climate change potential of 8.0 kg CO2-eq caused by the use of the decanter centrifuge for the separation. Slurry acidification showed a climate change potential that was slightly lower than the scenario without acidification for the in- house acidification scenario, and 60% higher for the field acidifi- cation scenario. In both acidification scenarios, extra GHG emis- sions were associated with the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3. In the case of in-house acidification, these emissions were counteracted by greater savings in wheat production elsewhere in the world and by decreased GHG emissions from slurry storage in animal housing and outdoor facilities. This was not the case for field acidification. Combining in-house acidification with separation resulted in a climate change potential 23% lower than for in-house acidification alone, whereas field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation resulted in a climate change potential that was

almost three times that of separation alone. Compared to the field acidification scenario without separation, the impact potential was 5 kg CO2-eq lower. The increase in climate change potential of 11 kg CO2-eq for field acidification compared to the reference scenario was smaller than the increase of 15 kg CO2-eq for field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation compared to separation alone.

Slurry acidification was the preferred technology for reducing terrestrial eutrophication potential, with impact potential de- creases of 31% for field acidification and 72% for in-house acidifi- cation compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2b). The terrestrial eutrophication potential was mainly caused by NH3 emissions, and as slurry acidification is mainly applied in order to decrease these emissions, this result was not surprising. In-house acidification in particular facilitated large decreases in NH3 emissions, starting from slurry storage in animal housing all the way to field applica- tion. Field acidification only decreased NH3 emissions during and shortly after field application. Slurry separation alone did not have an effect on the terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to the reference scenario, nor did separation of in-house acidified slurry affect the impact potential compared to in-house acidifica- tion without separation. However, separation combined with field acidification led to a slight increase in terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to field acidification without separation, mainly due to an increase in net field emissions.

For marine eutrophication potential, contributing and saving processes largely cancelled each other out, resulting in a total impact potential that was small for most scenarios (Fig. 2c). The positive and negative contributions were especially large for the in- house acidification scenario without separation. The slurry sepa- ration scenarios resulted in a slightly lower impact potential than the reference. The combination of in-house acidification and sep- aration in particular showed a decrease in marine eutrophication potential, while in-house acidification alone showed slight in- creases for this impact category. Net field emissions and replace- ment of winter wheat production elsewhere in the world were the main factors influencing marine eutrophication potential. The combination of in-house acidification and separation showed relatively low net field emissions compared to in-house acidifica- tion alone, regardless of the smaller savings accomplished from avoided wheat production.

Separation performed best for freshwater eutrophication po- tential, with a positive impact potential when slurry was not separated to a negative impact potential when it was separated with a decrease of ~171%. This decrease was mainly due to the assumption that the solid fraction was transported away from the pig farm to arable fields that were deficient in P. By doing this, it was assumed that the solid fraction was substituting mineral P fertiliser on these fields away from the farm, while mineral P fer- tiliser was assumed not to be replaced on the fields close to the farm, where unseparated slurry and the liquid fraction were applied. Slurry acidification without separation only had a minor effect on freshwater eutrophication potential due to savings from avoided wheat production. Combining separation with field acidi- fication led to a decrease in freshwater eutrophication potential comparable to separation alone of 173%, while in-house acidifica- tion and separation accomplished an impact potential that was 54% lower than without acidification and separation. The reason for this was that the separation efficiency for P was greatly decreased by acidification, as more P was made soluble when slurry was acidi- fied, and hence ended up in the liquid fraction.

Fossil resource depletion potential showed a negative impact potential for all scenarios, since resource savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser and avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world were greater than resource use for manure management, separation, acidification and tractor use (Fig. 2e). The

Fig. 2. Impact assessment of pig slurry separation and acidification scenarios under N regulation only, divided into stages. NS is no separation; S is separation; NA is no acidification; FA is field acidification; HA is in-house acidification.

largest savings were obtained for the reference scenario and the field acidification scenario without acidification. Compared to these two scenarios, fossil resource depletion potential was 17% higher for in-house acidification, 12% for separation, 17% for separation combined with field acidification, and 60% for in-house acidifica- tion combined with separation. Fossil resource consumption for acidification and separation were the main contributors to the smaller savings for the acidification and separation scenarios. For the two acidification scenarios, however, this was partly (in-house acidification) or completely (field acidification) counteracted by the greatest savings coming from avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world.

With respect to toxicity potential, two categories were influ- enced by slurry treatment, namely non-carcinogenic human toxicity and total ecotoxicity (Tables S8 & S9). Treatment by acidi- fication showed minor improvements for ecotoxicity potential, but

not for human toxicity potential. Slurry separation had a positive effect on toxicity potential, due to the larger area used for slurry spreading and the resulting lower copper and zinc concentrations. Separation and separation combined with field acidification led to 27% lower human toxicity potentials and 26% lower ecotoxicity potentials. In-house acidification combined with separation led to a 7% decrease in human toxicity and an 8% decrease in ecotoxicity.
3.2. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under N and P regulatory regimes

Introduction of more strict P regulations mainly led to decreased impact potentials for the scenarios without separation, namely the reference, field acidification and in-house acidification scenarios. The impact categories that had lower impact potential with P regulation in the scenarios without separation were climate change

potential, marine eutrophication potential, freshwater eutrophica- tion potential and fossil resource depletion potential (Fig. 3a, 3c, 3d & 3e). Only terrestrial eutrophication potential was barely affected by adjusting regulations to meet more strict P application limits (Fig. 3b, Tables S10 & S11). This impact potential decreased by 1% > - 5% for the reference and acidification-alon scenarios, whereas it increased by 10% for the in-house acidification combined with the separation scenario.

Stricter P regulations decreased the climate change potential for all scenarios that were limited by P application (i.e. all scenarios apart from separation only and separation with field acidification of the liquid fraction). The scenario with in-house acidification without separation showed the least impact potential of all sce- narios and the greatest decrease at 9.4 kg CO2-eq, followed by the

scenario with field acidification at 8.5 kg CO2-eq. The scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation showed a lower climate change potential when only N regulations were in place than in-house acidification without separation, but a higher impact potential with N and P regulations. One of the main causes of the decrease in climate change potential when P regulations were in place was the assumed replacement of mineral P fertiliser on the arable farm. With only N regulations, a major over-application of P on the pig farm occurred without replacement of mineral P fertil- iser. As P regulations limited the amount of slurry that could be applied on the pig farm, a proportion of slurry needed to be transported to and applied at an arable farm with a low soil P status, where it was assumed that this slurry P would replace mineral P fertiliser. The main increase in export of slurry to the arable farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser pig farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser arable farm

was shown for the reference scenario and the two acidification scenarios without separation. The decrease in climate change po- tential caused by replacement of mineral fertiliser was greater than the impact potential related to transportation of slurry, which was ~5 kg CO2-eq for the reference and acidification-only scenarios. Marine eutrophication potential decreased from ~0 kg N-eq with N application limits only, to -0.18 kg N-eq with both N and P application limits for the scenarios without treatment and with acidification only. This was mainly due to a decrease in nitrate leaching after field application of the slurry as a result of a lower N application rate, and to greater replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world. The impact potentials for scenarios with separation only or with separation combined with acidification were barely affected by the introduction of P regulations. When strict P application limits were in place, the beneficial effect of slurry separation on freshwater eutrophication potential diminished, as there was no over-application of P in the other scenarios, but rather a replacement of the required P on arable fields with a low P status in all the scenarios analysed. Approxi- mately the same amount of P was applied on the pig farm and arable farm in all scenarios, resulting in equal net field emissions and equal replacement of mineral P fertiliser. The main difference between scenarios was the replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, leading to a small variability in the net freshwater eutrophication potential. Fossil resource depletion potential decreased for all scenarios apart from separation alone and separation combined with field acidification of the liquid fraction when P regulations were intro- duced, compared to N regulation alone. Due to the larger trans- portation distance for slurry and acidified slurry that were field applied on an arable farm ~100 km away from the pig production, the positive fossil resource depletion potential was greater. How- ever, due to a larger replacement of mineral fertiliser production and more replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, net fossil resource depletion potential decreased. The reference scenario and scenario with field acidification of slurry remained the scenarios with the greatest negative fossil resource depletion po- tential, and the scenario with in-house acidification alone per- formed slightly worse than these two. However, a change in the relative order of scenarios occurred for the scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation, since with P regulations this scenario performed better than the other two separation sce- narios, while it performed worst of all scenarios with only N reg- ulations in place. Human toxicity potential (non-carcinogenic) and ecotoxicity potential were roughly the same for all scenarios, as the amounts of toxic substance application on field at the pig farm and at the arable farm were similar for all scenarios (Tables S10 & S11). The scenario with in-house acidification and separation performed slightly better than the other scenarios, due to the parting of Cu and Zn to the different fractions.

Slurry separation had a positive effect on toxicity potential, due to the larger area used for slurry spreading and the resulting lower copper and zinc concentrations. Separation and separation combined with field acidification led to 27% lower human toxicity potentials and 26% lower ecotoxicity potentials. In-house acidification combined with separation led to a 7% decrease in human toxicity and an 8% decrease in ecotoxicity.
3.2. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under N and P regulatory regimes

Introduction of more strict P regulations mainly led to decreased impact potentials for the scenarios without separation, namely the reference, field acidification and in-house acidification scenarios. The impact categories that had lower impact potential with P regulation in the scenarios without separation were climate change

potential, marine eutrophication potential, freshwater eutrophica- tion potential and fossil resource depletion potential (Fig. 3a, 3c, 3d & 3e). Only terrestrial eutrophication potential was barely affected by adjusting regulations to meet more strict P application limits (Fig. 3b, Tables S10 & S11). This impact potential decreased by 1% > - 5% for the reference and acidification-alon scenarios, whereas it increased by 10% for the in-house acidification combined with the separation scenario.

Stricter P regulations decreased the climate change potential for all scenarios that were limited by P application (i.e. all scenarios apart from separation only and separation with field acidification of the liquid fraction). The scenario with in-house acidification without separation showed the least impact potential of all sce- narios and the greatest decrease at 9.4 kg CO2-eq, followed by the

scenario with field acidification at 8.5 kg CO2-eq. The scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation showed a lower climate change potential when only N regulations were in place than in-house acidification without separation, but a higher impact potential with N and P regulations. One of the main causes of the decrease in climate change potential when P regulations were in place was the assumed replacement of mineral P fertiliser on the arable farm. With only N regulations, a major over-application of P on the pig farm occurred without replacement of mineral P fertil- iser. As P regulations limited the amount of slurry that could be applied on the pig farm, a proportion of slurry needed to be transported to and applied at an arable farm with a low soil P status, where it was assumed that this slurry P would replace mineral P fertiliser. The main increase in export of slurry to the arable farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser pig farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser arable farm

was shown for the reference scenario and the two acidification scenarios without separation. The decrease in climate change po- tential caused by replacement of mineral fertiliser was greater than the impact potential related to transportation of slurry, which was ~5 kg CO2-eq for the reference and acidification-only scenarios. Marine eutrophication potential decreased from ~0 kg N-eq with N application limits only, to -0.18 kg N-eq with both N and P application limits for the scenarios without treatment and with acidification only. This was mainly due to a decrease in nitrate leaching after field application of the slurry as a result of a lower N application rate, and to greater replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world. The impact potentials for scenarios with separation only or with separation combined with acidification were barely affected by the introduction of P regulations. When strict P application limits were in place, the beneficial effect of slurry separation on freshwater eutrophication potential diminished, as there was no over-application of P in the other scenarios, but rather a replacement of the required P on arable fields with a low P status in all the scenarios analysed. Approxi- mately the same amount of P was applied on the pig farm and arable farm in all scenarios, resulting in equal net field emissions and equal replacement of mineral P fertiliser. The main difference between scenarios was the replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, leading to a small variability in the net freshwater eutrophication potential. Fossil resource depletion potential decreased for all scenarios apart from separation alone and separation combined with field acidification of the liquid fraction when P regulations were intro- duced, compared to N regulation alone. Due to the larger trans- portation distance for slurry and acidified slurry that were field applied on an arable farm ~100 km away from the pig production, the positive fossil resource depletion potential was greater. How- ever, due to a larger replacement of mineral fertiliser production and more replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, net fossil resource depletion potential decreased. The reference scenario and scenario with field acidification of slurry remained the scenarios with the greatest negative fossil resource depletion po- tential, and the scenario with in-house acidification alone per- formed slightly worse than these two. However, a change in the relative order of scenarios occurred for the scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation, since with P regulations this scenario performed better than the other two separation sce- narios, while it performed worst of all scenarios with only N reg- ulations in place. Human toxicity potential (non-carcinogenic) and ecotoxicity potential were roughly the same for all scenarios, as the amounts of toxic substance application on field at the pig farm and at the arable farm were similar for all scenarios (Tables S10 & S11). The scenario with in-house acidification and separation performed slightly better than the other scenarios, due to the parting of Cu and Zn to the different fractions.
28: 3.1. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under an N regulatory regime

Pig slurry acidification and separation showed very different environmental impact potentials (Fig. 2). Slurry separation alone showed a climate change potential 50% lower than the scenario without separation and acidification (Fig. 2a). This decrease was mainly due to larger savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser in the case of slurry separation, which outweighed a positive contribution to climate change potential of 8.0 kg CO2-eq caused by the use of the decanter centrifuge for the separation. Slurry acidification showed a climate change potential that was slightly lower than the scenario without acidification for the in- house acidification scenario, and 60% higher for the field acidifi- cation scenario. In both acidification scenarios, extra GHG emis- sions were associated with the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3. In the case of in-house acidification, these emissions were counteracted by greater savings in wheat production elsewhere in the world and by decreased GHG emissions from slurry storage in animal housing and outdoor facilities. This was not the case for field acidification. Combining in-house acidification with separation resulted in a climate change potential 23% lower than for in-house acidification alone, whereas field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation resulted in a climate change potential that was

almost three times that of separation alone. Compared to the field acidification scenario without separation, the impact potential was 5 kg CO2-eq lower. The increase in climate change potential of 11 kg CO2-eq for field acidification compared to the reference scenario was smaller than the increase of 15 kg CO2-eq for field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation compared to separation alone.

Slurry acidification was the preferred technology for reducing terrestrial eutrophication potential, with impact potential de- creases of 31% for field acidification and 72% for in-house acidifi- cation compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2b). The terrestrial eutrophication potential was mainly caused by NH3 emissions, and as slurry acidification is mainly applied in order to decrease these emissions, this result was not surprising. In-house acidification in particular facilitated large decreases in NH3 emissions, starting from slurry storage in animal housing all the way to field applica- tion. Field acidification only decreased NH3 emissions during and shortly after field application. Slurry separation alone did not have an effect on the terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to the reference scenario, nor did separation of in-house acidified slurry affect the impact potential compared to in-house acidifica- tion without separation. However, separation combined with field acidification led to a slight increase in terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to field acidification without separation, mainly due to an increase in net field emissions.

For marine eutrophication potential, contributing and saving processes largely cancelled each other out, resulting in a total impact potential that was small for most scenarios (Fig. 2c). The positive and negative contributions were especially large for the in- house acidification scenario without separation. The slurry sepa- ration scenarios resulted in a slightly lower impact potential than the reference. The combination of in-house acidification and sep- aration in particular showed a decrease in marine eutrophication potential, while in-house acidification alone showed slight in- creases for this impact category. Net field emissions and replace- ment of winter wheat production elsewhere in the world were the main factors influencing marine eutrophication potential. The combination of in-house acidification and separation showed relatively low net field emissions compared to in-house acidifica- tion alone, regardless of the smaller savings accomplished from avoided wheat production.

Separation performed best for freshwater eutrophication po- tential, with a positive impact potential when slurry was not separated to a negative impact potential when it was separated with a decrease of ~171%. This decrease was mainly due to the assumption that the solid fraction was transported away from the pig farm to arable fields that were deficient in P. By doing this, it was assumed that the solid fraction was substituting mineral P fertiliser on these fields away from the farm, while mineral P fer- tiliser was assumed not to be replaced on the fields close to the farm, where unseparated slurry and the liquid fraction were applied. Slurry acidification without separation only had a minor effect on freshwater eutrophication potential due to savings from avoided wheat production. Combining separation with field acidi- fication led to a decrease in freshwater eutrophication potential comparable to separation alone of 173%, while in-house acidifica- tion and separation accomplished an impact potential that was 54% lower than without acidification and separation. The reason for this was that the separation efficiency for P was greatly decreased by acidification, as more P was made soluble when slurry was acidi- fied, and hence ended up in the liquid fraction.

Fossil resource depletion potential showed a negative impact potential for all scenarios, since resource savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser and avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world were greater than resource use for manure management, separation, acidification and tractor use (Fig. 2e). The

Fig. 2. Impact assessment of pig slurry separation and acidification scenarios under N regulation only, divided into stages. NS is no separation; S is separation; NA is no acidification; FA is field acidification; HA is in-house acidification.

largest savings were obtained for the reference scenario and the field acidification scenario without acidification. Compared to these two scenarios, fossil resource depletion potential was 17% higher for in-house acidification, 12% for separation, 17% for separation combined with field acidification, and 60% for in-house acidifica- tion combined with separation. Fossil resource consumption for acidification and separation were the main contributors to the smaller savings for the acidification and separation scenarios. For the two acidification scenarios, however, this was partly (in-house acidification) or completely (field acidification) counteracted by the greatest savings coming from avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world.

With respect to toxicity potential, two categories were influ- enced by slurry treatment, namely non-carcinogenic human toxicity and total ecotoxicity (Tables S8 & S9). Treatment by acidi- fication showed minor improvements for ecotoxicity potential, but

not for human toxicity potential. Slurry separation had a positive effect on toxicity potential, due to the larger area used for slurry spreading and the resulting lower copper and zinc concentrations. Separation and separation combined with field acidification led to 27% lower human toxicity potentials and 26% lower ecotoxicity potentials. In-house acidification combined with separation led to a 7% decrease in human toxicity and an 8% decrease in ecotoxicity.

potential, marine eutrophication potential, freshwater eutrophica- tion potential and fossil resource depletion potential (Fig. 3a, 3c, 3d & 3e). Only terrestrial eutrophication potential was barely affected by adjusting regulations to meet more strict P application limits (Fig. 3b, Tables S10 & S11). This impact potential decreased by 1% > - 5% for the reference and acidification-alon scenarios, whereas it increased by 10% for the in-house acidification combined with the separation scenario.

Stricter P regulations decreased the climate change potential for all scenarios that were limited by P application (i.e. all scenarios apart from separation only and separation with field acidification of the liquid fraction). The scenario with in-house acidification without separation showed the least impact potential of all sce- narios and the greatest decrease at 9.4 kg CO2-eq, followed by the

scenario with field acidification at 8.5 kg CO2-eq. The scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation showed a lower climate change potential when only N regulations were in place than in-house acidification without separation, but a higher impact potential with N and P regulations. One of the main causes of the decrease in climate change potential when P regulations were in place was the assumed replacement of mineral P fertiliser on the arable farm. With only N regulations, a major over-application of P on the pig farm occurred without replacement of mineral P fertil- iser. As P regulations limited the amount of slurry that could be applied on the pig farm, a proportion of slurry needed to be transported to and applied at an arable farm with a low soil P status, where it was assumed that this slurry P would replace mineral P fertiliser. The main increase in export of slurry to the arable farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser pig farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser arable farm

was shown for the reference scenario and the two acidification scenarios without separation. The decrease in climate change po- tential caused by replacement of mineral fertiliser was greater than the impact potential related to transportation of slurry, which was ~5 kg CO2-eq for the reference and acidification-only scenarios. Marine eutrophication potential decreased from ~0 kg N-eq with N application limits only, to -0.18 kg N-eq with both N and P application limits for the scenarios without treatment and with acidification only. This was mainly due to a decrease in nitrate leaching after field application of the slurry as a result of a lower N application rate, and to greater replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world. The impact potentials for scenarios with separation only or with separation combined with acidification were barely affected by the introduction of P regulations. When strict P application limits were in place, the beneficial effect of slurry separation on freshwater eutrophication potential diminished, as there was no over-application of P in the other scenarios, but rather a replacement of the required P on arable fields with a low P status in all the scenarios analysed. Approxi- mately the same amount of P was applied on the pig farm and arable farm in all scenarios, resulting in equal net field emissions and equal replacement of mineral P fertiliser. The main difference between scenarios was the replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, leading to a small variability in the net freshwater eutrophication potential. Fossil resource depletion potential decreased for all scenarios apart from separation alone and separation combined with field acidification of the liquid fraction when P regulations were intro- duced, compared to N regulation alone. Due to the larger trans- portation distance for slurry and acidified slurry that were field applied on an arable farm ~100 km away from the pig production, the positive fossil resource depletion potential was greater. How- ever, due to a larger replacement of mineral fertiliser production and more replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, net fossil resource depletion potential decreased. The reference scenario and scenario with field acidification of slurry remained the scenarios with the greatest negative fossil resource depletion po- tential, and the scenario with in-house acidification alone per- formed slightly worse than these two. However, a change in the relative order of scenarios occurred for the scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation, since with P regulations this scenario performed better than the other two separation sce- narios, while it performed worst of all scenarios with only N reg- ulations in place. Human toxicity potential (non-carcinogenic) and ecotoxicity potential were roughly the same for all scenarios, as the amounts of toxic substance application on field at the pig farm and at the arable farm were similar for all scenarios (Tables S10 & S11). The scenario with in-house acidification and separation performed slightly better than the other scenarios, due to the parting of Cu and Zn to the different fractions.
31: 3.1. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under an N regulatory regime

Pig slurry acidification and separation showed very different environmental impact potentials (Fig. 2). Slurry separation alone showed a climate change potential 50% lower than the scenario without separation and acidification (Fig. 2a). This decrease was mainly due to larger savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser in the case of slurry separation, which outweighed a positive contribution to climate change potential of 8.0 kg CO2-eq caused by the use of the decanter centrifuge for the separation. Slurry acidification showed a climate change potential that was slightly lower than the scenario without acidification for the in- house acidification scenario, and 60% higher for the field acidifi- cation scenario. In both acidification scenarios, extra GHG emis- sions were associated with the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3. In the case of in-house acidification, these emissions were counteracted by greater savings in wheat production elsewhere in the world and by decreased GHG emissions from slurry storage in animal housing and outdoor facilities. This was not the case for field acidification. Combining in-house acidification with separation resulted in a climate change potential 23% lower than for in-house acidification alone, whereas field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation resulted in a climate change potential that was

almost three times that of separation alone. Compared to the field acidification scenario without separation, the impact potential was 5 kg CO2-eq lower. The increase in climate change potential of 11 kg CO2-eq for field acidification compared to the reference scenario was smaller than the increase of 15 kg CO2-eq for field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation compared to separation alone.

Slurry acidification was the preferred technology for reducing terrestrial eutrophication potential, with impact potential de- creases of 31% for field acidification and 72% for in-house acidifi- cation compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2b). The terrestrial eutrophication potential was mainly caused by NH3 emissions, and as slurry acidification is mainly applied in order to decrease these emissions, this result was not surprising. In-house acidification in particular facilitated large decreases in NH3 emissions, starting from slurry storage in animal housing all the way to field applica- tion. Field acidification only decreased NH3 emissions during and shortly after field application. Slurry separation alone did not have an effect on the terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to the reference scenario, nor did separation of in-house acidified slurry affect the impact potential compared to in-house acidifica- tion without separation. However, separation combined with field acidification led to a slight increase in terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to field acidification without separation, mainly due to an increase in net field emissions.

For marine eutrophication potential, contributing and saving processes largely cancelled each other out, resulting in a total impact potential that was small for most scenarios (Fig. 2c). The positive and negative contributions were especially large for the in- house acidification scenario without separation. The slurry sepa- ration scenarios resulted in a slightly lower impact potential than the reference. The combination of in-house acidification and sep- aration in particular showed a decrease in marine eutrophication potential, while in-house acidification alone showed slight in- creases for this impact category. Net field emissions and replace- ment of winter wheat production elsewhere in the world were the main factors influencing marine eutrophication potential. The combination of in-house acidification and separation showed relatively low net field emissions compared to in-house acidifica- tion alone, regardless of the smaller savings accomplished from avoided wheat production.

Separation performed best for freshwater eutrophication po- tential, with a positive impact potential when slurry was not separated to a negative impact potential when it was separated with a decrease of ~171%. This decrease was mainly due to the assumption that the solid fraction was transported away from the pig farm to arable fields that were deficient in P. By doing this, it was assumed that the solid fraction was substituting mineral P fertiliser on these fields away from the farm, while mineral P fer- tiliser was assumed not to be replaced on the fields close to the farm, where unseparated slurry and the liquid fraction were applied. Slurry acidification without separation only had a minor effect on freshwater eutrophication potential due to savings from avoided wheat production. Combining separation with field acidi- fication led to a decrease in freshwater eutrophication potential comparable to separation alone of 173%, while in-house acidifica- tion and separation accomplished an impact potential that was 54% lower than without acidification and separation. The reason for this was that the separation efficiency for P was greatly decreased by acidification, as more P was made soluble when slurry was acidi- fied, and hence ended up in the liquid fraction.

Fossil resource depletion potential showed a negative impact potential for all scenarios, since resource savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser and avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world were greater than resource use for manure management, separation, acidification and tractor use (Fig. 2e). The

Fig. 2. Impact assessment of pig slurry separation and acidification scenarios under N regulation only, divided into stages. NS is no separation; S is separation; NA is no acidification; FA is field acidification; HA is in-house acidification.

largest savings were obtained for the reference scenario and the field acidification scenario without acidification. Compared to these two scenarios, fossil resource depletion potential was 17% higher for in-house acidification, 12% for separation, 17% for separation combined with field acidification, and 60% for in-house acidifica- tion combined with separation. Fossil resource consumption for acidification and separation were the main contributors to the smaller savings for the acidification and separation scenarios. For the two acidification scenarios, however, this was partly (in-house acidification) or completely (field acidification) counteracted by the greatest savings coming from avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world.

With respect to toxicity potential, two categories were influ- enced by slurry treatment, namely non-carcinogenic human toxicity and total ecotoxicity (Tables S8 & S9). Treatment by acidi- fication showed minor improvements for ecotoxicity potential, but

not for human toxicity potential. Slurry separation had a positive effect on toxicity potential, due to the larger area used for slurry spreading and the resulting lower copper and zinc concentrations. Separation and separation combined with field acidification led to 27% lower human toxicity potentials and 26% lower ecotoxicity potentials. In-house acidification combined with separation led to a 7% decrease in human toxicity and an 8% decrease in ecotoxicity.
