scenario with field acidification at 8.5 kg CO2-eq. The scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation showed a lower climate change potential when only N regulations were in place than in-house acidification without separation, but a higher impact potential with N and P regulations. One of the main causes of the decrease in climate change potential when P regulations were in place was the assumed replacement of mineral P fertiliser on the arable farm. With only N regulations, a major over-application of P on the pig farm occurred without replacement of mineral P fertil- iser. As P regulations limited the amount of slurry that could be applied on the pig farm, a proportion of slurry needed to be transported to and applied at an arable farm with a low soil P status, where it was assumed that this slurry P would replace mineral P fertiliser. The main increase in export of slurry to the arable farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser pig farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser arable farm

was shown for the reference scenario and the two acidification scenarios without separation. The decrease in climate change po- tential caused by replacement of mineral fertiliser was greater than the impact potential related to transportation of slurry, which was ~5 kg CO2-eq for the reference and acidification-only scenarios. Marine eutrophication potential decreased from ~0 kg N-eq with N application limits only, to -0.18 kg N-eq with both N and P application limits for the scenarios without treatment and with acidification only. This was mainly due to a decrease in nitrate leaching after field application of the slurry as a result of a lower N application rate, and to greater replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world. The impact potentials for scenarios with separation only or with separation combined with acidification were barely affected by the introduction of P regulations. When strict P application limits were in place, the beneficial effect of slurry separation on freshwater eutrophication potential diminished, as there was no over-application of P in the other scenarios, but rather a replacement of the required P on arable fields with a low P status in all the scenarios analysed. Approxi- mately the same amount of P was applied on the pig farm and arable farm in all scenarios, resulting in equal net field emissions and equal replacement of mineral P fertiliser. The main difference between scenarios was the replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, leading to a small variability in the net freshwater eutrophication potential. Fossil resource depletion potential decreased for all scenarios apart from separation alone and separation combined with field acidification of the liquid fraction when P regulations were intro- duced, compared to N regulation alone. Due to the larger trans- portation distance for slurry and acidified slurry that were field applied on an arable farm ~100 km away from the pig production, the positive fossil resource depletion potential was greater. How- ever, due to a larger replacement of mineral fertiliser production and more replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, net fossil resource depletion potential decreased. The reference scenario and scenario with field acidification of slurry remained the scenarios with the greatest negative fossil resource depletion po- tential, and the scenario with in-house acidification alone per- formed slightly worse than these two. However, a change in the relative order of scenarios occurred for the scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation, since with P regulations this scenario performed better than the other two separation sce- narios, while it performed worst of all scenarios with only N reg- ulations in place. Human toxicity potential (non-carcinogenic) and ecotoxicity potential were roughly the same for all scenarios, as the amounts of toxic substance application on field at the pig farm and at the arable farm were similar for all scenarios (Tables S10 & S11). The scenario with in-house acidification and separation performed slightly better than the other scenarios, due to the parting of Cu and Zn to the different fractions.
34: 3.1. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under an N regulatory regime

Pig slurry acidification and separation showed very different environmental impact potentials (Fig. 2). Slurry separation alone showed a climate change potential 50% lower than the scenario without separation and acidification (Fig. 2a). This decrease was mainly due to larger savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser in the case of slurry separation, which outweighed a positive contribution to climate change potential of 8.0 kg CO2-eq caused by the use of the decanter centrifuge for the separation. Slurry acidification showed a climate change potential that was slightly lower than the scenario without acidification for the in- house acidification scenario, and 60% higher for the field acidifi- cation scenario. In both acidification scenarios, extra GHG emis- sions were associated with the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3. In the case of in-house acidification, these emissions were counteracted by greater savings in wheat production elsewhere in the world and by decreased GHG emissions from slurry storage in animal housing and outdoor facilities. This was not the case for field acidification. Combining in-house acidification with separation resulted in a climate change potential 23% lower than for in-house acidification alone, whereas field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation resulted in a climate change potential that was

almost three times that of separation alone. Compared to the field acidification scenario without separation, the impact potential was 5 kg CO2-eq lower. The increase in climate change potential of 11 kg CO2-eq for field acidification compared to the reference scenario was smaller than the increase of 15 kg CO2-eq for field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation compared to separation alone.

Slurry acidification was the preferred technology for reducing terrestrial eutrophication potential, with impact potential de- creases of 31% for field acidification and 72% for in-house acidifi- cation compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2b). The terrestrial eutrophication potential was mainly caused by NH3 emissions, and as slurry acidification is mainly applied in order to decrease these emissions, this result was not surprising. In-house acidification in particular facilitated large decreases in NH3 emissions, starting from slurry storage in animal housing all the way to field applica- tion. Field acidification only decreased NH3 emissions during and shortly after field application. Slurry separation alone did not have an effect on the terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to the reference scenario, nor did separation of in-house acidified slurry affect the impact potential compared to in-house acidifica- tion without separation. However, separation combined with field acidification led to a slight increase in terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to field acidification without separation, mainly due to an increase in net field emissions.

For marine eutrophication potential, contributing and saving processes largely cancelled each other out, resulting in a total impact potential that was small for most scenarios (Fig. 2c). The positive and negative contributions were especially large for the in- house acidification scenario without separation. The slurry sepa- ration scenarios resulted in a slightly lower impact potential than the reference. The combination of in-house acidification and sep- aration in particular showed a decrease in marine eutrophication potential, while in-house acidification alone showed slight in- creases for this impact category. Net field emissions and replace- ment of winter wheat production elsewhere in the world were the main factors influencing marine eutrophication potential. The combination of in-house acidification and separation showed relatively low net field emissions compared to in-house acidifica- tion alone, regardless of the smaller savings accomplished from avoided wheat production.

Separation performed best for freshwater eutrophication po- tential, with a positive impact potential when slurry was not separated to a negative impact potential when it was separated with a decrease of ~171%. This decrease was mainly due to the assumption that the solid fraction was transported away from the pig farm to arable fields that were deficient in P. By doing this, it was assumed that the solid fraction was substituting mineral P fertiliser on these fields away from the farm, while mineral P fer- tiliser was assumed not to be replaced on the fields close to the farm, where unseparated slurry and the liquid fraction were applied. Slurry acidification without separation only had a minor effect on freshwater eutrophication potential due to savings from avoided wheat production. Combining separation with field acidi- fication led to a decrease in freshwater eutrophication potential comparable to separation alone of 173%, while in-house acidifica- tion and separation accomplished an impact potential that was 54% lower than without acidification and separation. The reason for this was that the separation efficiency for P was greatly decreased by acidification, as more P was made soluble when slurry was acidi- fied, and hence ended up in the liquid fraction.

Fossil resource depletion potential showed a negative impact potential for all scenarios, since resource savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser and avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world were greater than resource use for manure management, separation, acidification and tractor use (Fig. 2e). The

Fig. 2. Impact assessment of pig slurry separation and acidification scenarios under N regulation only, divided into stages. NS is no separation; S is separation; NA is no acidification; FA is field acidification; HA is in-house acidification.

largest savings were obtained for the reference scenario and the field acidification scenario without acidification. Compared to these two scenarios, fossil resource depletion potential was 17% higher for in-house acidification, 12% for separation, 17% for separation combined with field acidification, and 60% for in-house acidifica- tion combined with separation. Fossil resource consumption for acidification and separation were the main contributors to the smaller savings for the acidification and separation scenarios. For the two acidification scenarios, however, this was partly (in-house acidification) or completely (field acidification) counteracted by the greatest savings coming from avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world.

With respect to toxicity potential, two categories were influ- enced by slurry treatment, namely non-carcinogenic human toxicity and total ecotoxicity (Tables S8 & S9). Treatment by acidi- fication showed minor improvements for ecotoxicity potential, but

not for human toxicity potential. Slurry separation had a positive effect on toxicity potential, due to the larger area used for slurry spreading and the resulting lower copper and zinc concentrations. Separation and separation combined with field acidification led to 27% lower human toxicity potentials and 26% lower ecotoxicity potentials. In-house acidification combined with separation led to a 7% decrease in human toxicity and an 8% decrease in ecotoxicity.
3.2. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under N and P regulatory regimes

Introduction of more strict P regulations mainly led to decreased impact potentials for the scenarios without separation, namely the reference, field acidification and in-house acidification scenarios. The impact categories that had lower impact potential with P regulation in the scenarios without separation were climate change

potential, marine eutrophication potential, freshwater eutrophica- tion potential and fossil resource depletion potential (Fig. 3a, 3c, 3d & 3e). Only terrestrial eutrophication potential was barely affected by adjusting regulations to meet more strict P application limits (Fig. 3b, Tables S10 & S11). This impact potential decreased by 1% > - 5% for the reference and acidification-alon scenarios, whereas it increased by 10% for the in-house acidification combined with the separation scenario.

Stricter P regulations decreased the climate change potential for all scenarios that were limited by P application (i.e. all scenarios apart from separation only and separation with field acidification of the liquid fraction). scenario with field acidification at 8.5 kg CO2-eq. As P regulations limited the amount of slurry that could be applied on the pig farm, a proportion of slurry needed to be transported to and applied at an arable farm with a low soil P status, where it was assumed that this slurry P would replace mineral P fertiliser. The main increase in export of slurry to the arable farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser pig farm
Avoided production of mineral fertiliser arable farm

was shown for the reference scenario and the two acidification scenarios without separation. The decrease in climate change po- tential caused by replacement of mineral fertiliser was greater than the impact potential related to transportation of slurry, which was ~5 kg CO2-eq for the reference and acidification-only scenarios. Marine eutrophication potential decreased from ~0 kg N-eq with N application limits only, to -0.18 kg N-eq with both N and P application limits for the scenarios without treatment and with acidification only. This was mainly due to a decrease in nitrate leaching after field application of the slurry as a result of a lower N application rate, and to greater replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world. The impact potentials for scenarios with separation only or with separation combined with acidification were barely affected by the introduction of P regulations. When strict P application limits were in place, the beneficial effect of slurry separation on freshwater eutrophication potential diminished, as there was no over-application of P in the other scenarios, but rather a replacement of the required P on arable fields with a low P status in all the scenarios analysed. Approxi- mately the same amount of P was applied on the pig farm and arable farm in all scenarios, resulting in equal net field emissions and equal replacement of mineral P fertiliser. The main difference between scenarios was the replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, leading to a small variability in the net freshwater eutrophication potential. Fossil resource depletion potential decreased for all scenarios apart from separation alone and separation combined with field acidification of the liquid fraction when P regulations were intro- duced, compared to N regulation alone. Due to the larger trans- portation distance for slurry and acidified slurry that were field applied on an arable farm ~100 km away from the pig production, the positive fossil resource depletion potential was greater. How- ever, due to a larger replacement of mineral fertiliser production and more replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, net fossil resource depletion potential decreased. The reference scenario and scenario with field acidification of slurry remained the scenarios with the greatest negative fossil resource depletion po- tential, and the scenario with in-house acidification alone per- formed slightly worse than these two. However, a change in the relative order of scenarios occurred for the scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation, since with P regulations this scenario performed better than the other two separation sce- narios, while it performed worst of all scenarios with only N reg- ulations in place. Human toxicity potential (non-carcinogenic) and ecotoxicity potential were roughly the same for all scenarios, as the amounts of toxic substance application on field at the pig farm and at the arable farm were similar for all scenarios (Tables S10 & S11). The scenario with in-house acidification and separation performed slightly better than the other scenarios, due to the parting of Cu and Zn to the different fractions.
37: 3.1. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under an N regulatory regime

Pig slurry acidification and separation showed very different environmental impact potentials (Fig. 2). Slurry separation alone showed a climate change potential 50% lower than the scenario without separation and acidification (Fig. 2a). This decrease was mainly due to larger savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser in the case of slurry separation, which outweighed a positive contribution to climate change potential of 8.0 kg CO2-eq caused by the use of the decanter centrifuge for the separation. Slurry acidification showed a climate change potential that was slightly lower than the scenario without acidification for the in- house acidification scenario, and 60% higher for the field acidifi- cation scenario. In both acidification scenarios, extra GHG emis- sions were associated with the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3. In the case of in-house acidification, these emissions were counteracted by greater savings in wheat production elsewhere in the world and by decreased GHG emissions from slurry storage in animal housing and outdoor facilities. This was not the case for field acidification. Combining in-house acidification with separation resulted in a climate change potential 23% lower than for in-house acidification alone, whereas field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation resulted in a climate change potential that was

almost three times that of separation alone. Compared to the field acidification scenario without separation, the impact potential was 5 kg CO2-eq lower. The increase in climate change potential of 11 kg CO2-eq for field acidification compared to the reference scenario was smaller than the increase of 15 kg CO2-eq for field acidification of the liquid fraction after separation compared to separation alone.

Slurry acidification was the preferred technology for reducing terrestrial eutrophication potential, with impact potential de- creases of 31% for field acidification and 72% for in-house acidifi- cation compared to the reference scenario (Fig. 2b). The terrestrial eutrophication potential was mainly caused by NH3 emissions, and as slurry acidification is mainly applied in order to decrease these emissions, this result was not surprising. In-house acidification in particular facilitated large decreases in NH3 emissions, starting from slurry storage in animal housing all the way to field applica- tion. Field acidification only decreased NH3 emissions during and shortly after field application. Slurry separation alone did not have an effect on the terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to the reference scenario, nor did separation of in-house acidified slurry affect the impact potential compared to in-house acidifica- tion without separation. However, separation combined with field acidification led to a slight increase in terrestrial eutrophication potential compared to field acidification without separation, mainly due to an increase in net field emissions.

For marine eutrophication potential, contributing and saving processes largely cancelled each other out, resulting in a total impact potential that was small for most scenarios (Fig. 2c). The positive and negative contributions were especially large for the in- house acidification scenario without separation. The slurry sepa- ration scenarios resulted in a slightly lower impact potential than the reference. The combination of in-house acidification and sep- aration in particular showed a decrease in marine eutrophication potential, while in-house acidification alone showed slight in- creases for this impact category. Net field emissions and replace- ment of winter wheat production elsewhere in the world were the main factors influencing marine eutrophication potential. The combination of in-house acidification and separation showed relatively low net field emissions compared to in-house acidifica- tion alone, regardless of the smaller savings accomplished from avoided wheat production.

Separation performed best for freshwater eutrophication po- tential, with a positive impact potential when slurry was not separated to a negative impact potential when it was separated with a decrease of ~171%. This decrease was mainly due to the assumption that the solid fraction was transported away from the pig farm to arable fields that were deficient in P. By doing this, it was assumed that the solid fraction was substituting mineral P fertiliser on these fields away from the farm, while mineral P fer- tiliser was assumed not to be replaced on the fields close to the farm, where unseparated slurry and the liquid fraction were applied. Slurry acidification without separation only had a minor effect on freshwater eutrophication potential due to savings from avoided wheat production. Combining separation with field acidi- fication led to a decrease in freshwater eutrophication potential comparable to separation alone of 173%, while in-house acidifica- tion and separation accomplished an impact potential that was 54% lower than without acidification and separation. The reason for this was that the separation efficiency for P was greatly decreased by acidification, as more P was made soluble when slurry was acidi- fied, and hence ended up in the liquid fraction.

Fossil resource depletion potential showed a negative impact potential for all scenarios, since resource savings from avoided production of mineral fertiliser and avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world were greater than resource use for manure management, separation, acidification and tractor use (Fig. 2e). The

Fig. 2. Impact assessment of pig slurry separation and acidification scenarios under N regulation only, divided into stages. NS is no separation; S is separation; NA is no acidification; FA is field acidification; HA is in-house acidification.

largest savings were obtained for the reference scenario and the field acidification scenario without acidification. Compared to these two scenarios, fossil resource depletion potential was 17% higher for in-house acidification, 12% for separation, 17% for separation combined with field acidification, and 60% for in-house acidifica- tion combined with separation. Fossil resource consumption for acidification and separation were the main contributors to the smaller savings for the acidification and separation scenarios. For the two acidification scenarios, however, this was partly (in-house acidification) or completely (field acidification) counteracted by the greatest savings coming from avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world.

With respect to toxicity potential, two categories were influ- enced by slurry treatment, namely non-carcinogenic human toxicity and total ecotoxicity (Tables S8 & S9). Treatment by acidi- fication showed minor improvements for ecotoxicity potential, but

not for human toxicity potential. Slurry separation had a positive effect on toxicity potential, due to the larger area used for slurry spreading and the resulting lower copper and zinc concentrations. Separation and separation combined with field acidification led to 27% lower human toxicity potentials and 26% lower ecotoxicity potentials. In-house acidification combined with separation led to a 7% decrease in human toxicity and an 8% decrease in ecotoxicity.
3.2. Effects of slurry treatment by acidification and by separation under N and P regulatory regimes

Introduction of more strict P regulations mainly led to decreased impact potentials for the scenarios without separation, namely the reference, field acidification and in-house acidification scenarios. The impact categories that had lower impact potential with P regulation in the scenarios without separation were climate change

potential, marine eutrophication potential, freshwater eutrophica- tion potential and fossil resource depletion potential (Fig. 3a, 3c, 3d & 3e). Only terrestrial eutrophication potential was barely affected by adjusting regulations to meet more strict P application limits (Fig. 3b, Tables S10 & S11). This impact potential decreased by 1% > - 5% for the reference and acidification-alon scenarios, whereas it increased by 10% for the in-house acidification combined with the separation scenario.

Stricter P regulations decreased the climate change potential for all scenarios that were limited by P application (i.e. all scenarios apart from separation only and separation with field acidification of the liquid fraction). The scenario with in-house acidification without separation showed the least impact potential of all sce- narios and the greatest decrease at 9.4 kg CO2-eq, followed by the

scenario with field acidification at 8.5 kg CO2-eq. The scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation showed a lower climate change potential when only N regulations were in place than in-house acidification without separation, but a higher impact potential with N and P regulations. One of the main causes of the decrease in climate change potential when P regulations were in place was the assumed replacement of mineral P fertiliser on the arable farm. Marine eutrophication potential decreased from ~0 kg N-eq with N application limits only, to -0.18 kg N-eq with both N and P application limits for the scenarios without treatment and with acidification only. This was mainly due to a decrease in nitrate leaching after field application of the slurry as a result of a lower N application rate, and to greater replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world. The impact potentials for scenarios with separation only or with separation combined with acidification were barely affected by the introduction of P regulations. When strict P application limits were in place, the beneficial effect of slurry separation on freshwater eutrophication potential diminished, as there was no over-application of P in the other scenarios, but rather a replacement of the required P on arable fields with a low P status in all the scenarios analysed. Approxi- mately the same amount of P was applied on the pig farm and arable farm in all scenarios, resulting in equal net field emissions and equal replacement of mineral P fertiliser. The main difference between scenarios was the replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, leading to a small variability in the net freshwater eutrophication potential. Fossil resource depletion potential decreased for all scenarios apart from separation alone and separation combined with field acidification of the liquid fraction when P regulations were intro- duced, compared to N regulation alone. Due to the larger trans- portation distance for slurry and acidified slurry that were field applied on an arable farm ~100 km away from the pig production, the positive fossil resource depletion potential was greater. How- ever, due to a larger replacement of mineral fertiliser production and more replacement of wheat production elsewhere in the world, net fossil resource depletion potential decreased. The reference scenario and scenario with field acidification of slurry remained the scenarios with the greatest negative fossil resource depletion po- tential, and the scenario with in-house acidification alone per- formed slightly worse than these two. However, a change in the relative order of scenarios occurred for the scenario with in-house acidification combined with separation, since with P regulations this scenario performed better than the other two separation sce- narios, while it performed worst of all scenarios with only N reg- ulations in place. Human toxicity potential (non-carcinogenic) and ecotoxicity potential were roughly the same for all scenarios, as the amounts of toxic substance application on field at the pig farm and at the arable farm were similar for all scenarios (Tables S10 & S11). The scenario with in-house acidification and separation performed slightly better than the other scenarios, due to the parting of Cu and Zn to the different fractions.
40: Impact potentials from the separation only scenario and the scenario with field acidification of the liquid fraction were identical under N regulations only and under N and P regulations combined. In the case where the liquid fraction after separation was acidified during field application, impacts from three processes were affected compared to no acidification of the liquid fraction. These processes were the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3, net field emissions from the liquid fraction and avoided wheat pro- duction elsewhere in the world. The impacts associated with avoided wheat production were favourable when the liquid fraction was acidified during field application, as more wheat production

was replaced compared to when the liquid fraction was not acidi- fied. For climate change potential, contributing processes showed a larger impact potential than saving processes, resulting in an overall climate change potential that was 165% higher when the liquid fraction was acidified compared to no acidification of the liquid fraction (Fig. 2a). The opposite trend appeared for terrestrial eutrophication potential and marine eutrophication potential, meaning that impact potential savings from avoided production of wheat were greater than impact potentials associated with the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3 and net field emissions from the liquid fraction (Fig. 2b & 2c). This led to an 11% decrease in terrestrial eutrophication potential and a 10% decrease in marine eutrophication potential. Fossil resource depletion potential showed a slight increase of 4% (Fig. 2e). Freshwater eutrophication potential, human toxicity potential and ecotoxicity potential were not affected by liquid fraction acidification (Fig. 2d, Tables S8 & S9).

42: Separation after in-house acidification resulted in decreased impacts in all impact categories apart from fossil resource depletion compared with no separation after acidification when only N reg- ulations were followed (Fig. 2, Tables S8 & S9). Climate change potential showed a decrease of 23% in the case of separation after in-house acidification. This was mainly affected by lower net field emissions, larger savings in the production and use of mineral fertiliser, additional GHG emissions associated with the use of the decanter centrifuge, and smaller savings in wheat production elsewhere in the world. A decrease of 17% was shown for terrestrial eutrophication potential, mainly due to decreased NH3 emissions from field application of the liquid and solid fractions, compared to unseparated acidified slurry. For marine eutrophication potential, the improvement came from savings in nitrate leaching after field application of separated fractions of slurry compared to unsepa- rated slurry, and smaller savings in wheat production elsewhere in the world. The freshwater eutrophication potential was lower when slurry was separated after in-house acidification compared to no separation, due to the assumption that the solid fraction was replacing the production and use of mineral P fertiliser, while the liquid fraction and slurry were assumed not to replace mineral P fertiliser. This led to a 49% improvement in freshwater eutrophi- cation potential. Human toxicity potential and ecotoxicity potential both improved slightly, by ~7% for human toxicity and 6% for eco- toxicity, when in-house acidified slurry was separated into solid and liquid fractions compared to no separation. Fossil resource depletion potential was higher in the case where in-house acidified slurry was separated. The fossil resource depletion potential increased by a factor of two due to high energy use for slurry separation. Where both N and P regulations were applied, the combination of in-house acidification with separation showed comparable impact potentials as in-house acidification alone for all impact categories. The environmental benefits that were revealed under N regulation alone diminished with the addition of P regulation.
Sensitivity analysis was only run on scenarios under N regulation.

44: Adjusting NH3 emission coefficients mainly resulted in changes in terrestrial eutrophication potential, with low emission co- efficients leading to a decrease of 2.4 AE for the scenario without acidification and separation to 5.8 AE for the scenario with
NS NA NSFA NS HA SNA SFA SHA
Fig. 4. Sensitivity analysis for low and high NH3 emission coefficients. NS is no separation; S is separation; NA is no acidification; FA is field acidification; HA is in-house acidification.

separation and field acidification (Fig. 4b). High emission co- efficients led to increases in terrestrial eutrophication potential ranging from 2.5 AE > - < 5.7 AE. The relative order of scenarios changed with high emission coefficients, with the separation-only scenario being the worst performing. With low NH3 emission co- efficients, the climate change potential and marine eutrophication potential net field emissions increased for all scenarios, because less N was emitted as NH3, resulting in more N being left to be emitted as N20 and leached as NO3 (Fig. 4a & 4c). For the scenario without treatment and the separation-only scenario, these higher impact potentials were counteracted by savings through the avoi- ded production of mineral fertiliser and avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world, leading to a net lower impact potential for climate change and marine eutrophication with low NH3 emission coefficients. However, for climate change potential, the changes were small. For the in-house acidification scenario, climate change and marine eutrophication potential were higher with low emis- sion coefficients. High NH3 emission coefficients led to a decrease in marine eutrophication potential for the two acidification scenarios, while it led to an increase for all the other scenarios. High emission coefficients resulted in a greater impact potential from pig housing and smaller savings in wheat production elsewhere in the world, while net field emissions led to a smaller impact potential compared to the currently used values. High emission coefficients only slightly affected climate change potential for all the scenarios except for the in-house acidification scenario, where the impact potential decreased by 11%. Freshwater eutrophication potential and fossil resource depletion potential were affected slightly by adjusting NH3 emission coefficients, mainly due to changes in avoided wheat production elsewhere in the world, due to higher fertiliser value when NH3 emissions were set low (Fig. 4d & 4e). The relative order of scenarios was not affected for these two impact categories.

a greater increase than others (Fig. 5). For all scenarios NH3 emis- sions were the main contributor to indirect N20 emissions, and therefore the scenarios without acidification showed the largest increase in climate change potential when indirect N20 emissions were included. The impact potential for the separation-only sce- nario increased by 105%, while it increased by 61% for the scenario without separation and acidification. The in-house acidification scenarios showed an increase of 15% when no consecutive separation was undertaken, and a minor decrease when it was. 50: These results demonstrate that, depending on the regulatory regime and the impact category, one slurry treatment technology type may be preferable over another. Other LCA studies of slurry management have generally produced variable results with regard to whether slurry treatment is beneficial or not (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; Hamelin et al., 2011; De Vries et al., 2012; Croxatto Vega et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Whether a particular treatment pays off from an environmental perspective depends on the treatment technology and impact categories in focus. Generally, when bioenergy is produced, treat- ment appears more beneficial (Hamelin et al., 2011; Croxatto Vega et al., 2014) than without bioenergy production from manure (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012; ten Hoeve et al., 2014). For treatment technologies without bioenergy production, only slurry acidification appears environmentally beneficial (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). In both ten Hoeve et al. (2016) and the cur- rent study, it is shown that slurry acidification is the preferred technology for reducing terrestrial eutrophication potential. In accordance with ten Hoeve et al. (2014), slurry separation in this study performed best for freshwater eutrophication potential when only N regulations were considered. For climate change potential the effect of acidification depended on the technology (i.e. whether it happened in-house or in the field, with field acidification leading to an increase in climate change potential and in-house acidifica- tion leading to a small decrease for this impact category compared to the reference scenario). Slurry separation showed a comparable climate change potential to the scenario without treatment when both N and P regulations were in place, and a lower impact po- tential when only N regulation was in place. This last finding does not agree with findings by Hou et al. (2015), which state that slurry separation alone does not seem to impact NH3 and GHG emissions. The main difference between the current study and Hou et al. (2015) is the inclusion of secondary processes in this study (e.g. avoided wheat production elsewhere and avoided mineral fertiliser production and use).

Combinations of mitigation measures and treatment technolo- gies are increasingly being implemented to decrease environ- mental emissions of slurry management and highlight the importance of the investigation of proper combinations of mea- sures (Hou et al., 2015). In the current study, it appeared that the combination of in-house acidification and separation led to the greatest decrease in marine eutrophication potential of all sce- narios, whereas in-house acidification and separation alone only led to slight increases or decreases. However, this was only the case when N regulations alone were in place. Apparently, the beneficial effect of slurry separation was expressed in the HA scenario, while

the relative beneficial effect of avoided wheat production else- where in the world from the in-house acidification scenario was also maintained. However, marine eutrophication potential was close to zero for all scenarios, due to saving processes that showed the same magnitude as contributing processes. However, when both N and P regulations were in place, the marine eutrophication potential of in-house acidification without separation decreased to the same level as the combination of in-house acidification and separation, due to lower net field emissions when some of the acidified slurry was transported to and applied on an arable field. The results from the present study show that field acidification of the liquid fraction after slurry separation negatively affected climate change potential, regardless of the regulation in focus of only N regulations or both N and P regulations. This was due to higher N20 emissions after field application and additional emis- sions related to the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3.

With respect to P separation, slurry acidification prior to sepa- ration was unfavourable. For unacidified slurry, ~79% of total P ended up in the solid fraction, while this amount was only 23% for acidified slurry. The redistribution potential for acidified slurry was therefore much lower. This led to a higher freshwater eutrophica- tion potential for the combination of in-house acidification and separation compared to separation only. A decanter centrifuge was shown to be effective at allocating P to the solid fraction, especially for unacidified slurry. If there was only N regulation for the appli- cation of the solid fraction and no P limitations, P application would be 128 kg for unacidified solids. As this was assumed to be an unrealistic practice in the long run (i.e. a modelling period of 100 years), a P application limit of 30 kg ha was used. This implied that the area on which the solid fraction was spread increased from 98.3 m² to 459 m². For separation of in-house acidified slurry without P limitations, application of the solid fraction would lead to 54 kg P ha and the area would be 66.8 m². With a P limit of 30 kg ha- the area of application was almost double, at 121 m².

By acidifying slurry, farmers are able to maintain more N in the system, as less NH3 is volatilised (Kai et al., 2008), leading, among other things, to better yields, especially when strict N regulations are in place (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). The introduction of P appli- cation limits on top of the already existing N regulations led to a decrease in environmental impact potentials for the reference and acidification scenarios. Slurry separation was favourable with respect to P supplementation and lower P losses, as solid fractions were transported and applied to fields with a low soil P status under an N regulatory regime. However, these advantages of slurry separation disappeared when both N and P regulatory regimes were introduced, since transportation of slurry and its fractions to fields with a low soil P status would be required in all scenarios.

Both in-house acidification and separation of slurry are expen- sive treatments, for which the associated costs will often not be matched by savings from a more efficient use of the slurry (e.g. cost savings on the purchase of mineral fertilisers and from increased yields) (Sommer et al., 2015). Costs for field acidification were ex- pected to be lower than for in-house acidification, due to lower investment and running costs, since less acid and lime would be needed. It is debatable whether emission savings due to acidifica- tion and separation are worth the monetary investment. However, regulations can oblige farmers to alter slurry management. If re- strictions on NH3 emissions from livestock production were intro- duced, slurry acidification might be a feasible technology to apply, including from a financial perspective, whereas it appears that stricter P regulations would not generally lead to large savings for slurry acidification or separation, as the freshwater eutrophication potential is comparable for all analysed scenarios. It would be interesting to analyse the environmental consequences of diverting
livestock production to areas with a relatively low soil P status if P regulations were implemented.

Fangueiro et al. (2010) concluded that total N20 emissions could be decreased by > 30% after soil application when in-house acidi- fied slurry is separated. However, the opposite trend was found in the current study. The total N20 emission after field application of non-acidified slurry was 0.43 kg N20 or 128 kg CO2-eq. For the liquid fraction of in-house acidified and separated slurry it was 0.43 kg N20, and for the solid fraction it was 0.13 kg N20, resulting in total N20 emissions from the in-house acidified and separated fractions of 0.56 kg N20 or 166 kg CO2-eq. This meant an increase of 30% compared to the reference scenario. One reason for this discrepancy could be that the values in this study are for a 100-year modelling period, while Fangueiro et al. (2010) only considered a 61-day incubation study.

The use of lime (CaCO3), or at least the amount needed, is also debateable. In this study, a conservative estimate was made on the amount of lime added, namely 300 kg > - < 600 kg CaCO3 per hectare (Petersen and Sorensen, 2008). Climate change potential was the impact category that was mainly affected by the production and application of lime. If a less conservative assumption were made with respect to the addition of lime, the climate change po- tential of the scenarios with field acidification and in-house acidi- fication would decrease. The climate change potential for the field acidification scenarios, both with and without separation, would become more similar from the impact potential of the reference scenario. The performance of the in-house acidification scenarios, again with and without separation, would be even better for climate change potential compared to the reference scenario when a less conservative estimate of the amount of lime needed is applied.

In the sensitivity analysis, important parameters that affected the impact potentials were identified. Ammonia emission co- efficients from the slurry management chain influence terrestrial and marine eutrophication and climate change potential, through oxidation into N20 (Sommer et al., 2006; Nyord et al., 2013). This finding agrees with De Vries et al. (2012). Applying high NH3 emissions coefficients influenced the relative order of scenarios for terrestrial eutrophication potential, with the separation-only sce- nario being the worst performing. Phosphorus loss coefficients had an influence on the magnitude of freshwater eutrophication po- tential only, and the relative order of scenarios was unaffected, as also shown by ten Hoeve et al. (2014). Finally, the choice of inclu- sion or exclusion of indirect N20 emissions in the assessment was analysed. Some LCA studies on slurry management include indirect N20 emissions (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012, 2013; ten Hoeve et al., 2014), while others do not consider them (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). The current study showed that the choice of inclusion or exclusion influenced the ranking of scenarios. The separation-only scenario performed best when indirect N20 emissions were excluded from the analysis, while the two scenarios with in-house acidification performed better than the separation-only scenario when indirect N20 emis- sions were included. This was mainly due to the lower NH3 emis- sions in the in-house acidification scenarios with and without separation.

Slurry separation was favourable with respect to P supplementation and lower P losses, as solid fractions were transported and applied to fields with a low soil P status under an N regulatory regime. However, these advantages of slurry separation disappeared when both N and P regulatory regimes were introduced, since transportation of slurry and its fractions to fields with a low soil P status would be required in all scenarios.

Both in-house acidification and separation of slurry are expen- sive treatments, for which the associated costs will often not be matched by savings from a more efficient use of the slurry (e.g. cost savings on the purchase of mineral fertilisers and from increased yields) (Sommer et al., 2015). Costs for field acidification were ex- pected to be lower than for in-house acidification, due to lower investment and running costs, since less acid and lime would be needed. It is debatable whether emission savings due to acidifica- tion and separation are worth the monetary investment. However, regulations can oblige farmers to alter slurry management. If re- strictions on NH3 emissions from livestock production were intro- duced, slurry acidification might be a feasible technology to apply, including from a financial perspective, whereas it appears that stricter P regulations would not generally lead to large savings for slurry acidification or separation, as the freshwater eutrophication potential is comparable for all analysed scenarios. It would be interesting to analyse the environmental consequences of diverting
livestock production to areas with a relatively low soil P status if P regulations were implemented.

Fangueiro et al. (2010) concluded that total N20 emissions could be decreased by > 30% after soil application when in-house acidi- fied slurry is separated. However, the opposite trend was found in the current study. The total N20 emission after field application of non-acidified slurry was 0.43 kg N20 or 128 kg CO2-eq. For the liquid fraction of in-house acidified and separated slurry it was 0.43 kg N20, and for the solid fraction it was 0.13 kg N20, resulting in total N20 emissions from the in-house acidified and separated fractions of 0.56 kg N20 or 166 kg CO2-eq. This meant an increase of 30% compared to the reference scenario. One reason for this discrepancy could be that the values in this study are for a 100-year modelling period, while Fangueiro et al. (2010) only considered a 61-day incubation study.

The use of lime (CaCO3), or at least the amount needed, is also debateable. In this study, a conservative estimate was made on the amount of lime added, namely 300 kg > - < 600 kg CaCO3 per hectare (Petersen and Sorensen, 2008). Climate change potential was the impact category that was mainly affected by the production and application of lime. If a less conservative assumption were made with respect to the addition of lime, the climate change po- tential of the scenarios with field acidification and in-house acidi- fication would decrease. The climate change potential for the field acidification scenarios, both with and without separation, would become more similar from the impact potential of the reference scenario. The performance of the in-house acidification scenarios, again with and without separation, would be even better for climate change potential compared to the reference scenario when a less conservative estimate of the amount of lime needed is applied.

In the sensitivity analysis, important parameters that affected the impact potentials were identified. Ammonia emission co- efficients from the slurry management chain influence terrestrial and marine eutrophication and climate change potential, through oxidation into N20 (Sommer et al., 2006; Nyord et al., 2013). This finding agrees with De Vries et al. (2012). Applying high NH3 emissions coefficients influenced the relative order of scenarios for terrestrial eutrophication potential, with the separation-only sce- nario being the worst performing. Phosphorus loss coefficients had an influence on the magnitude of freshwater eutrophication po- tential only, and the relative order of scenarios was unaffected, as also shown by ten Hoeve et al. (2014). Finally, the choice of inclu- sion or exclusion of indirect N20 emissions in the assessment was analysed. Some LCA studies on slurry management include indirect N20 emissions (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012, 2013; ten Hoeve et al., 2014), while others do not consider them (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). The current study showed that the choice of inclusion or exclusion influenced the ranking of scenarios. The separation-only scenario performed best when indirect N20 emissions were excluded from the analysis, while the two scenarios with in-house acidification performed better than the separation-only scenario when indirect N20 emis- sions were included. This was mainly due to the lower NH3 emis- sions in the in-house acidification scenarios with and without separation.
52: These results demonstrate that, depending on the regulatory regime and the impact category, one slurry treatment technology type may be preferable over another. Other LCA studies of slurry management have generally produced variable results with regard to whether slurry treatment is beneficial or not (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; Hamelin et al., 2011; De Vries et al., 2012; Croxatto Vega et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Whether a particular treatment pays off from an environmental perspective depends on the treatment technology and impact categories in focus. Generally, when bioenergy is produced, treat- ment appears more beneficial (Hamelin et al., 2011; Croxatto Vega et al., 2014) than without bioenergy production from manure (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012; ten Hoeve et al., 2014). For treatment technologies without bioenergy production, only slurry acidification appears environmentally beneficial (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). In both ten Hoeve et al. (2016) and the cur- rent study, it is shown that slurry acidification is the preferred technology for reducing terrestrial eutrophication potential. In accordance with ten Hoeve et al. (2014), slurry separation in this study performed best for freshwater eutrophication potential when only N regulations were considered. For climate change potential the effect of acidification depended on the technology (i.e. whether it happened in-house or in the field, with field acidification leading to an increase in climate change potential and in-house acidifica- tion leading to a small decrease for this impact category compared to the reference scenario). Slurry separation showed a comparable climate change potential to the scenario without treatment when both N and P regulations were in place, and a lower impact po- tential when only N regulation was in place. This last finding does not agree with findings by Hou et al. (2015), which state that slurry separation alone does not seem to impact NH3 and GHG emissions. The main difference between the current study and Hou et al. (2015) is the inclusion of secondary processes in this study (e.g. avoided wheat production elsewhere and avoided mineral fertiliser production and use).

Combinations of mitigation measures and treatment technolo- gies are increasingly being implemented to decrease environ- mental emissions of slurry management and highlight the importance of the investigation of proper combinations of mea- sures (Hou et al., 2015). In the current study, it appeared that the combination of in-house acidification and separation led to the greatest decrease in marine eutrophication potential of all sce- narios, whereas in-house acidification and separation alone only led to slight increases or decreases. However, this was only the case when N regulations alone were in place. Apparently, the beneficial effect of slurry separation was expressed in the HA scenario, while

the relative beneficial effect of avoided wheat production else- where in the world from the in-house acidification scenario was also maintained. However, marine eutrophication potential was close to zero for all scenarios, due to saving processes that showed the same magnitude as contributing processes. However, when both N and P regulations were in place, the marine eutrophication potential of in-house acidification without separation decreased to the same level as the combination of in-house acidification and separation, due to lower net field emissions when some of the acidified slurry was transported to and applied on an arable field. The results from the present study show that field acidification of the liquid fraction after slurry separation negatively affected climate change potential, regardless of the regulation in focus of only N regulations or both N and P regulations. This was due to higher N20 emissions after field application and additional emis- sions related to the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3.

With respect to P separation, slurry acidification prior to sepa- ration was unfavourable. For unacidified slurry, ~79% of total P ended up in the solid fraction, while this amount was only 23% for acidified slurry. The redistribution potential for acidified slurry was therefore much lower. This led to a higher freshwater eutrophica- tion potential for the combination of in-house acidification and separation compared to separation only. A decanter centrifuge was shown to be effective at allocating P to the solid fraction, especially for unacidified slurry. If there was only N regulation for the appli- cation of the solid fraction and no P limitations, P application would be 128 kg for unacidified solids. As this was assumed to be an unrealistic practice in the long run (i.e. a modelling period of 100 years), a P application limit of 30 kg ha was used. This implied that the area on which the solid fraction was spread increased from 98.3 m² to 459 m². For separation of in-house acidified slurry without P limitations, application of the solid fraction would lead to 54 kg P ha and the area would be 66.8 m². With a P limit of 30 kg ha- the area of application was almost double, at 121 m².

The separation-only scenario performed best when indirect N20 emissions were excluded from the analysis, while the two scenarios with in-house acidification performed better than the separation-only scenario when indirect N20 emis- sions were included. This was mainly due to the lower NH3 emis- sions in the in-house acidification scenarios with and without separation.
55: These results demonstrate that, depending on the regulatory regime and the impact category, one slurry treatment technology type may be preferable over another. Other LCA studies of slurry management have generally produced variable results with regard to whether slurry treatment is beneficial or not (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; Hamelin et al., 2011; De Vries et al., 2012; Croxatto Vega et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Whether a particular treatment pays off from an environmental perspective depends on the treatment technology and impact categories in focus. Generally, when bioenergy is produced, treat- ment appears more beneficial (Hamelin et al., 2011; Croxatto Vega et al., 2014) than without bioenergy production from manure (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012; ten Hoeve et al., 2014). For treatment technologies without bioenergy production, only slurry acidification appears environmentally beneficial (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). In both ten Hoeve et al. (2016) and the cur- rent study, it is shown that slurry acidification is the preferred technology for reducing terrestrial eutrophication potential. In accordance with ten Hoeve et al. (2014), slurry separation in this study performed best for freshwater eutrophication potential when only N regulations were considered. For climate change potential the effect of acidification depended on the technology (i.e. whether it happened in-house or in the field, with field acidification leading to an increase in climate change potential and in-house acidifica- tion leading to a small decrease for this impact category compared to the reference scenario). Slurry separation showed a comparable climate change potential to the scenario without treatment when both N and P regulations were in place, and a lower impact po- tential when only N regulation was in place. This last finding does not agree with findings by Hou et al. (2015), which state that slurry separation alone does not seem to impact NH3 and GHG emissions. The main difference between the current study and Hou et al. (2015) is the inclusion of secondary processes in this study (e.g. avoided wheat production elsewhere and avoided mineral fertiliser production and use).

Combinations of mitigation measures and treatment technolo- gies are increasingly being implemented to decrease environ- mental emissions of slurry management and highlight the importance of the investigation of proper combinations of mea- sures (Hou et al., 2015). In the current study, it appeared that the combination of in-house acidification and separation led to the greatest decrease in marine eutrophication potential of all sce- narios, whereas in-house acidification and separation alone only led to slight increases or decreases. However, this was only the case when N regulations alone were in place. Apparently, the beneficial effect of slurry separation was expressed in the HA scenario, while

the relative beneficial effect of avoided wheat production else- where in the world from the in-house acidification scenario was also maintained. However, marine eutrophication potential was close to zero for all scenarios, due to saving processes that showed the same magnitude as contributing processes. However, when both N and P regulations were in place, the marine eutrophication potential of in-house acidification without separation decreased to the same level as the combination of in-house acidification and separation, due to lower net field emissions when some of the acidified slurry was transported to and applied on an arable field. The results from the present study show that field acidification of the liquid fraction after slurry separation negatively affected climate change potential, regardless of the regulation in focus of only N regulations or both N and P regulations. This was due to higher N20 emissions after field application and additional emis- sions related to the production and use of H2SO4 and CaCO3.

With respect to P separation, slurry acidification prior to sepa- ration was unfavourable. For unacidified slurry, ~79% of total P ended up in the solid fraction, while this amount was only 23% for acidified slurry. The redistribution potential for acidified slurry was therefore much lower. This led to a higher freshwater eutrophica- tion potential for the combination of in-house acidification and separation compared to separation only. A decanter centrifuge was shown to be effective at allocating P to the solid fraction, especially for unacidified slurry. If there was only N regulation for the appli- cation of the solid fraction and no P limitations, P application would be 128 kg for unacidified solids. As this was assumed to be an unrealistic practice in the long run (i.e. a modelling period of 100 years), a P application limit of 30 kg ha was used. This implied that the area on which the solid fraction was spread increased from 98.3 m² to 459 m². For separation of in-house acidified slurry without P limitations, application of the solid fraction would lead to 54 kg P ha and the area would be 66.8 m². With a P limit of 30 kg ha- the area of application was almost double, at 121 m².

By acidifying slurry, farmers are able to maintain more N in the system, as less NH3 is volatilised (Kai et al., 2008), leading, among other things, to better yields, especially when strict N regulations are in place (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). The introduction of P appli- cation limits on top of the already existing N regulations led to a decrease in environmental impact potentials for the reference and acidification scenarios. Slurry separation was favourable with respect to P supplementation and lower P losses, as solid fractions were transported and applied to fields with a low soil P status under an N regulatory regime. However, these advantages of slurry separation disappeared when both N and P regulatory regimes were introduced, since transportation of slurry and its fractions to fields with a low soil P status would be required in all scenarios.

Both in-house acidification and separation of slurry are expen- sive treatments, for which the associated costs will often not be matched by savings from a more efficient use of the slurry (e.g. cost savings on the purchase of mineral fertilisers and from increased yields) (Sommer et al., 2015). Costs for field acidification were ex- pected to be lower than for in-house acidification, due to lower investment and running costs, since less acid and lime would be needed. It is debatable whether emission savings due to acidifica- tion and separation are worth the monetary investment. However, regulations can oblige farmers to alter slurry management. If re- strictions on NH3 emissions from livestock production were intro- duced, slurry acidification might be a feasible technology to apply, including from a financial perspective, whereas it appears that stricter P regulations would not generally lead to large savings for slurry acidification or separation, as the freshwater eutrophication potential is comparable for all analysed scenarios. It would be interesting to analyse the environmental consequences of diverting
livestock production to areas with a relatively low soil P status if P regulations were implemented.

Fangueiro et al. (2010) concluded that total N20 emissions could be decreased by > 30% after soil application when in-house acidi- fied slurry is separated. However, the opposite trend was found in the current study. The total N20 emission after field application of non-acidified slurry was 0.43 kg N20 or 128 kg CO2-eq. For the liquid fraction of in-house acidified and separated slurry it was 0.43 kg N20, and for the solid fraction it was 0.13 kg N20, resulting in total N20 emissions from the in-house acidified and separated fractions of 0.56 kg N20 or 166 kg CO2-eq. This meant an increase of 30% compared to the reference scenario. One reason for this discrepancy could be that the values in this study are for a 100-year modelling period, while Fangueiro et al. (2010) only considered a 61-day incubation study.

The use of lime (CaCO3), or at least the amount needed, is also debateable. In this study, a conservative estimate was made on the amount of lime added, namely 300 kg > - < 600 kg CaCO3 per hectare (Petersen and Sorensen, 2008). Climate change potential was the impact category that was mainly affected by the production and application of lime. If a less conservative assumption were made with respect to the addition of lime, the climate change po- tential of the scenarios with field acidification and in-house acidi- fication would decrease. The climate change potential for the field acidification scenarios, both with and without separation, would become more similar from the impact potential of the reference scenario. The performance of the in-house acidification scenarios, again with and without separation, would be even better for climate change potential compared to the reference scenario when a less conservative estimate of the amount of lime needed is applied.

In the sensitivity analysis, important parameters that affected the impact potentials were identified. Ammonia emission co- efficients from the slurry management chain influence terrestrial and marine eutrophication and climate change potential, through oxidation into N20 (Sommer et al., 2006; Nyord et al., 2013). This finding agrees with De Vries et al. (2012). Applying high NH3 emissions coefficients influenced the relative order of scenarios for terrestrial eutrophication potential, with the separation-only sce- nario being the worst performing. Phosphorus loss coefficients had an influence on the magnitude of freshwater eutrophication po- tential only, and the relative order of scenarios was unaffected, as also shown by ten Hoeve et al. (2014). Finally, the choice of inclu- sion or exclusion of indirect N20 emissions in the assessment was analysed. The redistribution potential for acidified slurry was therefore much lower. This led to a higher freshwater eutrophica- tion potential for the combination of in-house acidification and separation compared to separation only. A decanter centrifuge was shown to be effective at allocating P to the solid fraction, especially for unacidified slurry. If there was only N regulation for the appli- cation of the solid fraction and no P limitations, P application would be 128 kg for unacidified solids. As this was assumed to be an unrealistic practice in the long run (i.e. a modelling period of 100 years), a P application limit of 30 kg ha was used. This implied that the area on which the solid fraction was spread increased from 98.3 m² to 459 m². For separation of in-house acidified slurry without P limitations, application of the solid fraction would lead to 54 kg P ha and the area would be 66.8 m². With a P limit of 30 kg ha- the area of application was almost double, at 121 m².

By acidifying slurry, farmers are able to maintain more N in the system, as less NH3 is volatilised (Kai et al., 2008), leading, among other things, to better yields, especially when strict N regulations are in place (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). The introduction of P appli- cation limits on top of the already existing N regulations led to a decrease in environmental impact potentials for the reference and acidification scenarios. Slurry separation was favourable with respect to P supplementation and lower P losses, as solid fractions were transported and applied to fields with a low soil P status under an N regulatory regime. However, these advantages of slurry separation disappeared when both N and P regulatory regimes were introduced, since transportation of slurry and its fractions to fields with a low soil P status would be required in all scenarios.

Both in-house acidification and separation of slurry are expen- sive treatments, for which the associated costs will often not be matched by savings from a more efficient use of the slurry (e.g. cost savings on the purchase of mineral fertilisers and from increased yields) (Sommer et al., 2015). Costs for field acidification were ex- pected to be lower than for in-house acidification, due to lower investment and running costs, since less acid and lime would be needed. It is debatable whether emission savings due to acidifica- tion and separation are worth the monetary investment. However, regulations can oblige farmers to alter slurry management. If re- strictions on NH3 emissions from livestock production were intro- duced, slurry acidification might be a feasible technology to apply, including from a financial perspective, whereas it appears that stricter P regulations would not generally lead to large savings for slurry acidification or separation, as the freshwater eutrophication potential is comparable for all analysed scenarios. It would be interesting to analyse the environmental consequences of diverting
livestock production to areas with a relatively low soil P status if P regulations were implemented.

Fangueiro et al. (2010) concluded that total N20 emissions could be decreased by > 30% after soil application when in-house acidi- fied slurry is separated. However, the opposite trend was found in the current study. The total N20 emission after field application of non-acidified slurry was 0.43 kg N20 or 128 kg CO2-eq. For the liquid fraction of in-house acidified and separated slurry it was 0.43 kg N20, and for the solid fraction it was 0.13 kg N20, resulting in total N20 emissions from the in-house acidified and separated fractions of 0.56 kg N20 or 166 kg CO2-eq. This meant an increase of 30% compared to the reference scenario. One reason for this discrepancy could be that the values in this study are for a 100-year modelling period, while Fangueiro et al. (2010) only considered a 61-day incubation study.

The use of lime (CaCO3), or at least the amount needed, is also debateable. In this study, a conservative estimate was made on the amount of lime added, namely 300 kg > - < 600 kg CaCO3 per hectare (Petersen and Sorensen, 2008). Climate change potential was the impact category that was mainly affected by the production and application of lime. If a less conservative assumption were made with respect to the addition of lime, the climate change po- tential of the scenarios with field acidification and in-house acidi- fication would decrease. The climate change potential for the field acidification scenarios, both with and without separation, would become more similar from the impact potential of the reference scenario. The performance of the in-house acidification scenarios, again with and without separation, would be even better for climate change potential compared to the reference scenario when a less conservative estimate of the amount of lime needed is applied.

In the sensitivity analysis, important parameters that affected the impact potentials were identified. Ammonia emission co- efficients from the slurry management chain influence terrestrial and marine eutrophication and climate change potential, through oxidation into N20 (Sommer et al., 2006; Nyord et al., 2013). This finding agrees with De Vries et al. (2012). Applying high NH3 emissions coefficients influenced the relative order of scenarios for terrestrial eutrophication potential, with the separation-only sce- nario being the worst performing. Phosphorus loss coefficients had an influence on the magnitude of freshwater eutrophication po- tential only, and the relative order of scenarios was unaffected, as also shown by ten Hoeve et al. (2014). Finally, the choice of inclu- sion or exclusion of indirect N20 emissions in the assessment was analysed. Some LCA studies on slurry management include indirect N20 emissions (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012, 2013; ten Hoeve et al., 2014), while others do not consider them (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). The current study showed that the choice of inclusion or exclusion influenced the ranking of scenarios. The separation-only scenario performed best when indirect N20 emissions were excluded from the analysis, while the two scenarios with in-house acidification performed better than the separation-only scenario when indirect N20 emis- sions were included. This was mainly due to the lower NH3 emis- sions in the in-house acidification scenarios with and without separation.
59: Slurry acidification alone led to decreased environmental bur- dens, due to decreases in NH3 emissions in particular. In-house acidification especially led to decreased NH3 emissions throughout the whole manure management chain. With just N regulations, slurry separation alone led to equal, higher or lower impact potentials compared to no separation, depending on the

impact category in focus. However, the environmental benefits of separation disappeared after introduction of stricter P regulations because this would force farmers to transport and apply slurry to fields with a low P status even without separation. As trans- portation had relatively small environmental impacts compared to slurry storage and application, this did not present a problem in environmental terms. However, transportation might be expensive, which could force farmers to invest in separation technology any- way. In-house slurry acidification appeared to be the most benefi- cial scenario under P regulations. The combination of pig slurry separation and field acidification could be interesting if there were requirements for both NH3 emission decreases and P loss, but no strict P application limits. A synergetic positive effect appeared in relation to marine eutrophication potential for the combination of in-house acidification and separation, compared to these two technologies individually. The model was sensitive to the chosen NH3 emission coefficients and the choice to include indirect N20 emissions, expressed by a change in the order of scenarios for certain impact categories.
